cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the question. I need to find out what the Indians believed based on the letter they wrote. The question is asking what the Indians believed according to the letter. The letter says that the officials from Maryland and Virginia invited the Indians of the Six Nations to send their boys to William and Mary College. But the Indians refused. They said they know that the officials have a high opinion of their kind of learning and that the cost would be expensive, but they have a different way of looking at things. They explain that some of their young people were educated in the northern provinces, learned all the sciences, but when they came back, they were bad at living in the woods and were "totally good for nothing." The Indians then say that they are thankful for the offer but ask the officials to send their sons instead, and they would teach them to make them men. Now, the question is asking what the Indians believed. The options are A, B, C, D. Option A says they thought it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling. But the letter is refusing the offer, saying that their education makes the young people useless for their way of life. So maybe they don't think it's better for their boys to go to the colleges. Option B says they felt insulted by the offer. The letter says they are not offended and acknowledge the officials' good intentions, so probably not B. Option C says they knew more about science than the officials. The letter doesn't say that. They mention that the officials' colleges teach sciences, but the problem is about the practical skills, not the scientific knowledge. So probably not C. Option D says they had a better way of educating young men. The letter says
